glossary.def: A network that processes a sequence one step at a time, maintaining a hidden state that summarizes everything seen so far and updating it with the same shared weights at every step. Unrolling the recurrence over T steps yields a depth-T feed-forward network whose layers share parameters.

The vector an RNN carries from one time step to the next as a running summary of the past; in EQ N3.1 it is updated by h_t = tanh(W_xh x_t + W_hh h_{t-1} + b). In an LSTM the hidden state is a gated, squashed view of a separate cell state, while in a GRU the hidden state is the only memory.

The failure mode of training deep or recurrent nets where the backpropagated gradient is a long product of Jacobians (EQ N3.2) that shrinks toward zero or grows without bound geometrically with distance. Vanishing gradients silently blind an RNN to long-range dependencies; exploding gradients are curable by norm clipping, but vanishing ones need an architectural fix.

A gated recurrent cell (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) that adds a cell state updated mostly additively, with three sigmoid gates — forget, input, output — controlling what to erase, write, and read. The additive cell path forms a 'constant error carousel' whose near-identity gradient defeats the vanishing-gradient problem.

A sigmoid-valued vector in (0,1) that acts as a soft, differentiable valve on a memory channel: 0 blocks it, 1 passes it untouched. An LSTM has three gates (forget, input, output); a GRU has two (update, reset). The candidate (a tanh) is content, not a gate.

The LSTM's second memory track c_t, updated as c_t = f_t ⊙ c_{t-1} + i_t ⊙ c~_t. When the forget gate is near 1 and the input gate near 0 the cell is approximately unchanged, so the gradient flows back along it multiplied by ~1 instead of a contracting Jacobian — the mechanism that lets gradients survive across many steps.

A lighter gated cell (Cho et al., 2014) with a single state vector and two gates: an update gate that interpolates between keeping the old state and overwriting it with a candidate, and a reset gate that controls how much past state feeds the candidate. It merges the LSTM's forget and input gates into one and drops the separate cell state and output gate.

Training an RNN by unrolling it into its depth-T feed-forward equivalent and running ordinary backpropagation, summing each shared weight's gradient over all steps (EQ N3.7). Truncated BPTT cuts the sequence into chunks, backpropagating only within a chunk while carrying the hidden state forward, to bound memory and tame the gradient pathologies.

refs.cite: Hochreiter, S. & Schmidhuber, J. (1997). Long Short-Term Memory. Neural Computation 9(8).

Cho, K., van Merrienboer, B., Gulcehre, C., Bahdanau, D., Bougares, F., Schwenk, H. & Bengio, Y. (2014). Learning Phrase Representations using RNN Encoder-Decoder for Statistical Machine Translation. EMNLP 2014.
Bengio, Y., Simard, P. & Frasconi, P. (1994). Learning Long-Term Dependencies with Gradient Descent is Difficult. IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks 5(2).
Pascanu, R., Mikolov, T. & Bengio, Y. (2013). On the Difficulty of Training Recurrent Neural Networks. ICML 2013.
Greff, K., Srivastava, R. K., Koutnik, J., Steunebrink, B. R. & Schmidhuber, J. (2017). LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey. IEEE TNNLS 28(10).
Chung, J., Gulcehre, C., Cho, K. & Bengio, Y. (2014). Empirical Evaluation of Gated Recurrent Neural Networks on Sequence Modeling. NIPS 2014 Deep Learning Workshop.
Gu, A. & Dao, T. (2023). Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces. arXiv 2312.00752.
